content.claim: The United States conducted the longest and largest-scale rural school consolidation (reducing public schools from 248,117 in 1929–30 to 98,271 in 2013–14), driven by economies-of-scale rationale within locally elected school boards with independent taxing power, a fundamentally different institutional logic and governance environment than centralized systems
content.context: Fresh live research through the International comparison lens
limitations: US institutional context (locally elected boards, independent taxation, decentralized curriculum) is not transferable to centralized systems like post-2013 Hungary
Consolidation rationale prioritized aggregate system efficiency rather than village survival or depopulation prevention, limiting relevance to European retention frameworks
No evidence presented on long-term depopulation or community outcomes resulting from US consolidation
content.population: Population described in the cited finding
content.time_scope: As reported by the cited source
